turns.content: I'm trying to understand variance again, but I keep thinking PhantomData<T> is just a marker like in C++ templates. Does it actually store anything?
Great question. It's not storing data; it's purely a compile-time trick to tell the compiler how types relate, so you can safely use it without allocating memory on the heap.
So if I want contravariance with functions, is there a special PhantomData variant for that?
Yes. For functions you use PhantomData<fn(T)> to express contravariance, which lets you write traits where an impl trait can be used as a supertype.
Can I mix that with regular PhantomData<T> for covariance?
Absolutely. You can combine them in the same struct, so your trait impls can support both directions depending on what you're modeling.
What about invariance? Is there a way to say it doesn't change at all?
For true invariance you use PhantomData<*mut T>, which tells the compiler that your type is invariant and won't depend on any generic parameter.
So I can have a struct with multiple Phantoms to control variance for different fields?
Exactly. You can layer them together, so one field uses contravariance via PhantomData<fn(T)> while another uses covariance or invariance as needed.
Does this help with unsafe code too?
Yes, because the compiler guarantees that variance is correct at compile time, you can rely on it when writing safe wrappers around raw pointers or externs.
I think I get it now. Thanks for breaking down the variants so clearly.
You're welcome! If you want to try building a trait with all three variance types, feel free to ask and we can walk through the code together.

I've been trying to build a generic validator tool, and I keep running into issues with how generics interact with trait bounds. It feels exactly like the difference between covariant and contravariant functions in C++, where you can pass a more specific type but not a less specific one depending on direction. In Rust, I'm seeing PhantomData<fn(T)> seem to handle this kind of variance check differently than just using a simple marker. When I tried to use it with a trait object like &dyn Trait, the compiler was complaining about the lifetime elision being ambiguous because I didn't explicitly specify which variance side I needed.

That ambiguity you're hitting is classic when mixing up how PhantomData behaves with function pointers versus raw pointers. For covariant positions, like arguments to a trait method that takes a generic T, you'd typically use PhantomData<T> so the compiler knows T is fully known and cannot be substituted out. If you need invariance instead, which prevents any substitution entirely, then you must use PhantomData<*mut T>. The tricky part with contravariance using PhantomData<fn(T)> is that it's specifically designed to model functions where the argument type can be widened but not narrowed. So if your validator trait requires a specific input signature, wrapping it in PhantomData<fn()> tells the compiler you expect exactly that function shape and won't allow generic widening of the arguments.

I think I misunderstood how contravariance works there. When we say a type is contravariant in a trait position, like an argument to a method, we usually mean it can be replaced with a supertype, not narrowed. So if I have a function that takes &dyn Trait and I pass a concrete implementation of Trait, that should work fine because the concrete implements the dyn trait. But with PhantomData<fn(T)>, it seems like you're saying it prevents widening? That sounds contradictory to what I learned about variance rules.

You are correct about standard contravariance in traits where a supertype substitution is allowed, but PhantomData<fn(T)> isn't modeling the standard argument position of a trait method. Instead, it's modeling a function pointer type itself which acts as an immutable container for that specific function signature. When you use PhantomData<fn(T)>, you are essentially telling the compiler that this field holds a concrete function pointer and cannot be swapped with any other generic function pointer. This is useful when building a registry of validators where each entry must match a precise function signature exactly, preventing accidental widening to a more general function type.

Okay, so I need to distinguish between using PhantomData for marking lifetime boundaries versus marking variance constraints on actual data types. If I'm just storing a value and don't care about function pointers, I should stick with standard covariant PhantomData<T>. But if I want to enforce that a specific function signature is locked in place without allowing any generic substitution, then PhantomData<fn(T)> becomes necessary to ensure the type system sees it as an invariant container for that exact function.

Exactly. Standard PhantomData<T> tells the compiler the generic T is known and fixed, which covers most basic cases where you just need a marker for lifetime or data presence without worrying about variance rules. However, when your design requires strict enforcement of a specific function signature to prevent accidental widening or substitution, that's where PhantomData<fn(T)> comes in. It acts as an invariant wrapper specifically for function pointer types. You would see this pattern most often in libraries like serde_json::Value when handling serialized data structures, or in configuration systems using crates like config-rs where you need to ensure specific validation functions are locked into place.

It makes sense now. But I'm still confused about the raw pointer case with PhantomData<*mut T>. Why would anyone use that for invariance? Isn't a mutable pointer already quite flexible because you can mutate through it? If I just want to say this field cannot be genericized at all, why not just use a static marker type instead of involving pointers and lifetimes.

The raw pointer case with PhantomData<*mut T> is actually about expressing invariance relative to the data's lifetime rather than its concrete value. Unlike PhantomData<T> which assumes you know exactly what T is, or PhantomData<fn(T)> which locks a function signature, PhantomData<*mut T> signals that the field holds raw memory access where no generic substitution of the underlying type is allowed because the pointer itself doesn't carry type information about T. This is critical in unsafe code blocks within your validator tool when you need to ensure the compiler knows exactly what data lives at that address without allowing it to be swapped for a different generic type.

I see, so if I'm building this validator and one of the checks involves reading raw memory buffers from an external system, then PhantomData<*mut T> ensures that specific buffer pointer cannot be replaced with another arbitrary pointer. But what if I don't need raw pointers at all? Can I still achieve invariance without involving *mut or fn types? Or is variance really only expressible through these three specific patterns: standard generic markers, function pointers, and raw pointers.

You cannot express pure invariance without one of those three mechanisms because Rust's type system doesn't have a built-in 'invariant marker' like some other languages. If you need to prevent any substitution of the underlying data type entirely, you must use PhantomData<*mut T> for raw memory contexts or PhantomData<fn(T)> for function signature contexts. Standard covariant markers simply don't exist in Rust's design; everything is either covariant by default (like regular generics) or requires explicit contravariance markers like function pointers to flip the direction of substitution rules.

So my understanding is that if I want to model a scenario where a generic parameter T can never be substituted with any other type, I have no choice but to use either PhantomData<*mut T> or PhantomData<fn(T)>. There isn't a simple 'invariant marker' like there is for covariance.

That's exactly right. Rust forces you to pick the variance mechanism that matches your semantic need rather than providing a neutral invariance flag. If you're working with function signatures, PhantomData<fn(T)> locks the argument type. If you're dealing with raw memory or opaque handles where no type information exists beyond the pointer itself, PhantomData<*mut T> prevents substitution. This is why advanced variance patterns are so important when building complex generic systems like your validator tool.

This explains why I was getting errors earlier when I tried to use a simple marker for invariance. The compiler refused it because Rust doesn't support neutral markers, only specific variance mechanisms tied to function pointers or raw pointers. Now I can refactor my code to explicitly choose the right PhantomData variant based on whether I'm dealing with functions or raw memory access.

Perfect. That's exactly what you needed to do. By choosing the correct PhantomData variant—either PhantomData<fn(T)> for function signatures or PhantomData<*mut T> for raw pointers—you ensure your validator tool correctly enforces the intended variance constraints without relying on assumptions that Rust simply cannot make.

One last thing, I noticed that in some crates like serde_json::Value, they use these patterns internally to handle serialization. Does using PhantomData<fn(T)> there actually help with deserialization speed or does it just add a compile-time safety check without runtime cost? Is there any performance penalty for choosing the more specific variance markers over generic ones?

There is no runtime performance penalty for using these specific variance markers because they are purely compile-time constructs. PhantomData<fn(T)> and PhantomData<*mut T> do not allocate memory or add overhead at runtime like a boxed data structure would. They simply tell the compiler how to interpret generic parameters during type checking. Your validator tool will see no difference in execution speed whether you use standard covariant markers or these strict variance markers, as long as your logic remains sound.

That's reassuring. So I don't need to worry about micro-optimizations regarding which PhantomData variant I choose; the compiler will just handle it correctly if I follow the semantic rules. One final question: are there any new crates specifically designed to help developers manage these complex variance patterns, or do I have to implement all of this logic manually in my tool?

There aren't many specialized crates that automate variance management because understanding PhantomData<fn(T)> and PhantomData<*mut T> is fundamental knowledge required for advanced Rust development rather than a niche library feature. You will need to implement these patterns manually when building complex generic systems like your validator tool, but the clarity you've gained from this discussion should make it much easier to write correct code without needing extra dependencies.
I'm back with Rust after five years, and I've been staring at the variance docs. What's the actual difference between PhantomData<fn(T)>, PhantomData<T>, and PhantomData<*mut T>?

Welcome back! That's a great place to start because that specific mix of types is where most people get confused. The short answer is: PhantomData<fn(T)> handles contravariance, while plain PhantomData<T> handles covariance, and PhantomData<*mut T> represents invariance. You can think of it as the compiler using those markers to tell the borrow checker exactly how the generic parameter behaves.
Okay, so if I use PhantomData<T>, that means the type is covariant? And if I want contravariance, do I have to write a function pointer?

Basically yes. When you need contravariance—where a subtype can be used where a supertype is expected in certain trait contexts—the compiler needs that specific marker. You'd use PhantomData<fn(T)> to tell the type system that T appears in a position requiring contravariant bounds.
That sounds weird. I thought variance was just about whether a trait bound gets looser or tighter. Why does Rust need these phantom types?

Great question. The real reason is that in Rust's type system, you can't express contravariance in the standard way you might expect with just trait bounds alone. To get truly generic code that respects those rules without losing safety, the compiler uses these phantom markers to encode the variance direction into the type itself.
So PhantomData<T> is covariance and PhantomData<fn(T)> is contravariance? Is there an invariance marker too?

Exactly. You're already on the right track. Plain PhantomData<T> signals covariant behavior, while PhantomData<fn(T)> signals contravariant behavior. For invariance—where T doesn't change its relationship regardless of substitution—you use PhantomData<*mut T>. That tells the borrow checker that the type is invariant.
Wait, so if I'm writing a trait where the generic parameter appears in an output position, I need contravariance? And if it's in input positions, covariance?

You've got that backwards. If the generic parameter appears in an output position of a function or trait, you typically need contravariance because you're consuming the type. Conversely, if it's in input positions where you're producing from T, you generally use covariant bounds with PhantomData<T>.
Can I mix these? Like, have a struct that uses both covariance and contravariance for different generics?

Yes, absolutely. You can layer them. For example, you might define a trait where one generic uses PhantomData<T> for covariance while another uses PhantomData<fn(U)> for contravariance. The compiler allows this as long as each variant is correctly specified.
Does this affect how I write tests? Or do I just need to be careful with lifetimes?

It mostly affects how you define traits and structs, but it does influence your test cases too. You'll want to ensure your test data respects the variance rules defined by those phantom markers. Lifetimes still matter, especially if the generic types are tied to them.
What about serialization? If I'm using serde_json::Value with these variants, will it break?

Serde doesn't directly care about phantom types since they're erased at runtime. However, if you try to serialize a type that relies on those markers without proper trait implementations, serde_json::Value might not handle it gracefully unless you've implemented the necessary traits.
So I shouldn't worry about PhantomData<fn(T)> breaking my serialization pipeline?
Right. As long as your types implement the necessary traits for serde, the phantom markers won't cause issues at runtime. They're purely compile-time signals.
Thanks! This is actually clearer than I expected. Just one more thing: can these be used with foreign crates or macros?

Yes, they work fine with macros and external crates as long as the macro expansions don't inadvertently alter the variance rules you've set up. Most standard crates like serde_json::Value will integrate without problems.

I'm working on a generic container for my side project and I keep seeing references to PhantomData<fn(T)> and PhantomData<T>. What's the actual difference between using PhantomData with a function pointer versus just a regular type parameter?

The core distinction is how variance is handled. PhantomData<T> creates a covariant relationship, meaning if T becomes more specific it fits the trait better, while PhantomData<fn(T)> enforces contravariance where the function signature determines compatibility in the opposite direction of the type parameter.
Wait, so that means if I have a function taking &str and another taking &Vec<String>, those are different? Can't they both be used interchangeably with covariant types?

You're hitting the key point about contravariance. With PhantomData<fn(T)> the trait bound requires that any function implementing your interface must accept arguments of type T or something more generic, so &str and &Vec<String> aren't interchangeable here because they represent different levels of specificity in a contravariant context.
That sounds restrictive. Does this mean I can't use PhantomData<fn(T)> for my generic struct if I want to allow any type parameter?

Not necessarily, it depends on what you're trying to achieve. If your goal is to constrain the trait implementation based on function signatures rather than just data types, then yes, PhantomData<fn(T)> forces that specific variance behavior which might limit flexibility compared to using PhantomData<T> for simple covariance.
I see. But what about invariance? I've read somewhere that PhantomData<*mut T> is used for something else entirely, right?

Exactly, PhantomData<*mut T> represents invariance where the type parameter doesn't change at all regardless of specialization. This is useful when you need to ensure that a trait implementation works identically for any concrete type without considering variance relationships.
So if I'm building something like an iterator adapter where the output depends on input type, should I use PhantomData<fn(T)> or just regular PhantomData<T>?

For an iterator adapter where the transformation logic matters more than raw data types, you'd likely need PhantomData<fn(T)> to enforce that your functions can only operate on compatible types based on their signatures.

I've been back at coding for five years and I'm struggling to understand how variance actually works with PhantomData. Specifically, why does PhantomData<fn(T)> show contravariance while PhantomData<T> shows covariance? It feels like the documentation is too abstract and doesn't explain the real-world use cases where this matters.

That's a great question, and it's totally normal to feel confused when you first revisit these concepts. The key difference lies in how the generic parameter is used inside the struct. When you define PhantomData<fn(T)>, the function pointer acts as an input to the trait bound, which flips the variance direction compared to a regular type T. This is why it's contravariant: if you can pass a value of type T where fn(T) expects something, you can also pass a subtype of T because functions accept inputs broadly.

So the function pointer changes the variance from covariance to contravariance? But I still don't see how this helps me in my proof-of-concept. Can you give me a concrete example where I'd actually need PhantomData<fn(T)> versus just PhantomData<T>?

Imagine you're building a library that accepts different trait implementations for validation purposes. If your struct requires an object-safe trait implementation, using PhantomData<T> works fine because T is covariant here. But if you need to implement a trait like FnTrait where the generic parameter represents an input function, then PhantomData<fn(T)> becomes necessary to express contravariance correctly.
Got it, but what about invariance? When would I use PhantomData<*mut T> instead of the other two?

PhantomData<*mut T> expresses invariance because a raw pointer doesn't have variance—it's always invariant regardless of what T is. This is useful when you want to signal that your struct contains only opaque data with no concrete type relationship, like a wrapper around some internal state where the exact type doesn't matter for trait bounds.

That makes sense. So I should use PhantomData<T> when T is covariant, PhantomData<fn(T)> when I need contravariance related to inputs, and PhantomData<*mut T> for invariance scenarios? Is there a crate or macro that simplifies this?

There isn't a single crate that automates all of these cases because they depend on your specific trait bounds. However, you might find crates like derive_more helpful if you're using macros to generate boilerplate code for common patterns involving PhantomData. Still, understanding the underlying variance mechanics is crucial since Rust's compiler enforces strict rules based on how types are used in traits.
Thanks! I think I finally get why these distinctions matter. One last thing—does this affect lifetime annotations too, or do they stay separate?

Lifetimes and variance are related but distinct concepts. While lifetimes constrain how long a reference can live, variance controls how generic types behave in trait bounds. You can have both together—for example, PhantomData<T> with lifetime 'a—but they serve different purposes. In your proof-of-concept, focus on getting the variance right first, then add lifetimes as needed for safety.

I'm trying to build a generic container that holds closures, but I think if I use PhantomData<fn(T)> inside my struct, the compiler will let me store any function regardless of its return type because variance makes it covariant with respect to the argument list. Can you help me understand why this intuition is wrong?

You are assuming that storing a function pointer or closure in a field with a generic parameter T would make the struct covariant, but Rust actually treats PhantomData<fn(T)> as contravariant. This means that if you have two types where one takes more arguments than the other, a container holding the first cannot necessarily hold the second because the stored function must be callable with exactly those arguments. The compiler enforces this strictness to prevent unsafe aliasing of functions that might return different values or take different parameters.

So if I try to make a wrapper around fn(i32) -> u32 and another around fn(u64) -> i64, the first one will not accept the second one even though they seem similar? Is there a way to bypass this using PhantomData<T> instead of PhantomData<fn(T)>?

Exactly, that is the core distinction. PhantomData<T> creates covariance where T varies, allowing you to store different concrete types as long as their generic parameter matches, but it does not help with function signatures because functions are contravariant in their parameters and covariant in their return type only if the entire signature is invariant. When you mix these directions, Rust defaults to invariance for safety unless you explicitly use PhantomData<*mut T> which allows raw pointer aliasing but cannot hold actual closures safely.

I see, so the issue isn't just about the generic parameter T itself but how the function's input and output relate to variance. If I want a truly flexible container for any closure, is there a trait or macro that can automate this variance handling? Or do I have to manually manage lifetimes and type parameters?

There is no blanket solution in Rust because variance behavior is baked into the type system itself rather than being configurable via macros. You must explicitly choose between covariant, contravariant, or invariant behavior by selecting the correct PhantomData variant for your specific use case. If you need to store different closure types without violating safety guarantees, you will likely find that invariance with PhantomData<*mut T> is the only path forward, though it requires careful lifetime management.

Okay, so I should stop thinking about generic variance as something I can tweak dynamically and instead focus on defining my structs with the correct variance constraints upfront. Does this mean I cannot write a single struct that handles both returning different types and taking different arguments without separate implementations?

Precisely. The design of Rust's type system forces you to commit to one variance model per generic parameter, so yes, you will typically need separate implementations or trait bounds for each distinct combination of input parameters and output types if your application requires it. This limitation ensures that the compiler can reason about function calls without accidentally allowing incompatible function pointers to alias.

That makes sense now. I was trying to force Rust into a covariance mode where it would accept any function, but the reality is that Rust's invariance and contravariance rules protect against exactly those kinds of errors by refusing to let unrelated functions coexist in the same generic container.

You have hit on the key insight. The compiler refuses to allow arbitrary aliasing because it cannot guarantee that a function stored in one slot is safe to call with arguments intended for another slot. By understanding how PhantomData<fn(T)> enforces contravariance and how PhantomData<T> enables covariance, you can now design your generic containers intentionally rather than guessing at the type system's behavior.

I'm trying to model a generic function that accepts any trait object, but I keep running into variance issues when using PhantomData with closures. Specifically, I need to use PhantomData<fn(T)> for contravariance in my implementation of the Into trait.

That's a tricky problem because Rust's type system doesn't support generic functions directly like Java's wildcards. When you need to represent a function pointer or closure that accepts arguments, you typically use PhantomData<fn(T)> to achieve the contravariant bound required by traits like Into. However, there are limitations with how this interacts with lifetime elision and generic parameters.

Right, so I'm trying to write a wrapper that can hold any function pointer but needs to implement Into<dyn Fn()>. My current approach uses PhantomData<fn()> but the compiler complains about variance. Is there a way to make this work without explicit lifetime annotations?

The issue is that PhantomData<T> implies covariance while you need contravariance for function pointers in this context. You cannot simply use PhantomData<fn()> because it doesn't capture the argument types correctly. Instead, you might consider using a different strategy like opaque types or separate trait implementations rather than relying on variance alone.
Wait, I thought PhantomData was enough for this? Isn't it designed specifically to handle these generic cases without adding extra constraints?

You're right that PhantomData is useful, but its behavior depends heavily on the type parameter. PhantomData<T> provides covariance, whereas PhantomData<fn(T)> gives you contravariance only when T is a function pointer or closure. For your specific case involving Into<dyn Fn()>, you'll need to carefully consider whether the variance direction matches your trait bounds.
So if I use PhantomData<fn()> it won't work at all for contravariant bounds? Does that mean I have to abandon this approach entirely?

Not necessarily. You could explore using PhantomData<*mut T> which represents invariance, or consider structuring your design around separate trait implementations instead of trying to force a single generic implementation through variance.
What about using PhantomData with some concrete type as the placeholder? Like PhantomData<Option<fn()>>?

That won't help because Option changes the variance properties entirely. You'd be introducing covariance from the Option wrapper which conflicts with what you need for contravariance in function traits.
I see. So maybe I should just avoid trying to make a generic Into<dyn Fn()> at all? Just use concrete types instead?

That's often the pragmatic solution in Rust. Instead of fighting with variance constraints, you might define separate implementations for specific function pointer types or closures that implement your trait individually.
Okay, but what if I really need a generic interface? Is there any way to combine PhantomData with other mechanisms?
You could combine PhantomData with opaque types or use traits that abstract away the variance requirements. However, these approaches often come with performance penalties or complexity trade-offs.
So the bottom line is that PhantomData alone isn't enough for this specific use case?

Exactly. For contravariant bounds involving function pointers, you typically need either careful type specialization or alternative design patterns beyond relying solely on variance through PhantomData.
Got it. One more thing though - does this affect how I handle lifetimes in my generic wrapper?

Yes, lifetime elision rules interact differently with PhantomData<fn(T)> versus other variants. You may need explicit lifetime annotations to ensure the function pointer's lifetime aligns with your trait implementation.

Hey, I'm reviewing some code and I thought I saw a case where PhantomData<T> could actually make a trait bound covariant instead of invariant? I thought the docs said it was always invariant so I must have misread something.

You definitely didn't misread the documentation; that is exactly why you are here. PhantomData<T> is strictly covariant, while invariance applies to PhantomData<*mut T>. The confusion likely stems from how variance interacts with trait object bounds like fn(T) which creates contravariance scenarios.

Ah okay so if I use a function pointer inside the generic type it flips to contravariant? That sounds useful because I want to accept any higher-ranked trait object but only implement a specific bound for one of them.

That is correct. When you have PhantomData<fn(T)>, the variance becomes contravariant, which means it allows accepting more specialized types than generic ones in certain contexts like FnMut or FnOnce bounds where subtyping works differently.
I see. So when I tried to use a trait object as the type parameter for my struct, it failed because the compiler thinks I'm trying to implement a bound that requires invariance but got covariance?

Not quite; you actually hit an error because the compiler was enforcing invariance on PhantomData<*mut T> where your code required covariant behavior. You need to switch to using PhantomData<T> or specifically PhantomData<fn(T)> depending on whether you want covariance or contravariance.
Right, so the key distinction is that if I want a trait object bound like for T: Trait, then I can't use invariance because it won't allow me to substitute generic types with specialized ones.

Exactly. The compiler will reject your code if you try to use PhantomData<*mut T> when you need a type that allows substitution of generics for specialized versions, because invariance prevents that substitution entirely.
So the solution is to just change my struct definition to use PhantomData<T> instead? That should make it covariant and allow me to accept any implementation of Trait?

Yes, changing your struct to use PhantomData<T> will resolve the issue. This makes the type parameter covariant, allowing you to satisfy trait bounds with specialized implementations without restriction.
Wait, but what if I need both covariance and contravariance in the same codebase? Is there a way to combine them or do I have separate structs?

You cannot combine variance directions within a single generic type parameter. You must choose between covariant PhantomData<T>, contravariant PhantomData<fn(T)>, or invariant PhantomData<*mut T> based on your specific needs.
Got it, so there's no magic way to make a single type parameter do both? That makes sense because variance is fundamentally about how types relate to each other.
Correct. Variance determines how generic types can be substituted with specialized ones, and you must choose the appropriate direction for your specific use case.
One last thing, what happens if I try to pass a concrete type like String instead of a trait object? Does the variance still apply?

Yes, variance applies regardless of whether you use a concrete type or a trait object. The compiler checks the variance constraints on the type parameter itself, not just how it's used in your function.

I'm trying to implement a generic container that accepts any function pointer, but I keep hitting issues with variance when using PhantomData. Can you help me understand why my trait bound isn't working as expected?

Hey there! It's totally normal to get stuck on this when moving from Java generics to Rust's type system. The issue is likely that you're treating function pointers like regular types without considering variance rules. In Rust, PhantomData<fn(T)> specifically encodes contravariance for function types, which means the generic parameter appears in the input position of the function signature. This creates a constraint where functions must accept arguments from your inner type but can return anything. If you're seeing errors about bounds not being satisfied, it's probably because you're expecting covariance when your design actually requires contravariance.

Ah okay so that explains why my tests are failing, but I'm still confused about the difference between this and regular PhantomData<T> which seems to imply covariance. Why does Rust require me to use different variants for different variance directions?

Great question! The distinction comes down to how variance affects type safety and subtyping relationships. Regular PhantomData<T> signals that T is covariant, meaning if you have a subtype of T, it's also a valid substitute in place of the generic parameter. However, for function pointers where parameters come first, contravariance makes more sense because a broader input type can satisfy stricter requirements while narrower inputs cannot. That's why PhantomData<fn(T)> is needed to explicitly declare that your generic parameter appears as an argument position rather than a return position.
I understand the logic now but I'm still struggling with invariance cases. When do I actually need to use PhantomData<*mut T> instead of one of those variance-specific variants?

You hit on something important! Invariance happens when neither covariant nor contravariant behavior applies, usually because the type relationship doesn't preserve order or directionality in a way that supports subtyping. This occurs frequently with raw pointers like PhantomData<*mut T>. Unlike regular types where you can narrow down to specific implementations, mutable pointers don't have meaningful subtype relationships in Rust's type system since they represent memory addresses rather than values. When your generic container needs to hold anything regardless of its internal structure without assuming any ordering or compatibility guarantees, invariance is the only safe choice.

That makes sense about raw pointers but I'm wondering if there are practical examples where I'd make this mistake? Like maybe when trying to write a function that accepts different sized data structures?

Exactly! A common pitfall happens when you try to create a trait object or generic wrapper expecting all implementations to be interchangeable without considering their internal variance. For instance, if you define a Trait<T> where T represents various data structures and then try to store function pointers that operate on those types, you might accidentally assume covariance exists when it doesn't. Rust will catch this because the compiler knows that mixing different sized or differently structured data with mutable pointer constraints requires invariance to prevent unsafe memory access patterns.
